chapters.label: Full lesson narration
chapters.script:
A lender funds loans in a warehouse before selling them into a term deal. The loans are earning, but the warehouse bank, servicer, representations, and repurchase promises already determine who carries the early risk.

Warehouse, Servicing Advances, and Repurchase Risk begins with a defined cash right, not a product name. The relevant collateral is assets before and after term securitisation. That means the analyst must identify who owes the money, what contract creates the obligation, which account receives it, which party services or manages it, and what the investor can claim when the ordinary payment fails.

Warehouse facilities finance loan production before securitisation. Servicing advances keep note payments or property expenses moving when borrowers pay late. Representations, warranties, eligibility, repurchase, cure, substitution, and indemnity provisions allocate defects and losses between parties. The first useful model is a cash timeline: origination or purchase, scheduled collection, fees and permitted expenses, reserve or enhancement, note payment, principal allocation, and residual cash. If the timing changes, the investor’s return changes even when ultimate principal is eventually recovered.

A practical example makes the boundary visible. Imagine a pool with one hundred units of eligible collateral and notes supported by a defined portion of those collections. A payment arrives early, a payment arrives late, and a payment is disputed. The transaction documents decide whether each event changes principal, interest, reserve, enhancement, or only reporting. No single average can answer all three questions.

Warehouse agreements, sale contracts, servicing agreements, repurchase reports, exception files, and trustee data show whether an asset was eligible and whether a remedy can be collected. Public summaries rarely establish operational capacity or disputed claims. The source record supports the mechanism and the questions an investor should ask. It does not prove a new transaction’s pool performance, legal transfer, servicing quality, guarantee scope, or executable waterfall. Those missing facts remain diligence items rather than invitations to invent a conclusion.

A lender can originate too quickly, a borrower can default before take-out, collateral can breach a representation, or the warehouse lender can reduce funding. A servicer may advance cash and later discover recovery is uncertain. The failure path matters because a shortfall can begin as borrower behaviour, asset condition, administrative error, market liquidity, legal challenge, counterparty weakness, or management choice. The same label can therefore contain different risks depending on the contract, jurisdiction, data, and operating party.

Haircuts, margin calls, reserves, seller interests, repurchase obligations, indemnities, replacement servicers, and advance-reimbursement rules address different exposures. A legal right to repurchase is only as valuable as the seller’s solvency and the defect evidence. Protection should be described by the problem it solves. Credit enhancement absorbs value loss. Liquidity support bridges timing. A reserve supplies defined cash. Subordination changes loss priority. A guarantee transfers a claim to another counterparty. None is infinite, and none should be counted twice.

Trace an asset from origination to warehouse to term pool. Ask when funding is called, when a defect is found, who advances cash, when the advance is reimbursed, and whether the remedy survives the seller’s failure. The interview-quality decision is to state the cash engine, the first failure bearer, the protection available, and the evidence still required. Ask what changes if the payment is early, late, reduced, redirected, or never arrives. Then ask whether the remedy is cash, time, legal enforcement, or only a revised forecast.

A disciplined review would build a small failure ledger for warehouse, servicing advances, and repurchase risk. The first column names the payment source and legal obligor. The second records the servicing, management, collection, or collateral process that must work. The third identifies the test, reserve, guarantee, or priority rule that responds. The fourth states what the investor actually receives after delay, expense, loss, or counterparty failure. This keeps a clean balance-sheet description from being mistaken for a cash-flow conclusion.

The same ledger should carry the limit of the evidence. Pool data can support performance analysis; a legal opinion can support a transfer question; a trustee report can support a payment history; an offering document can define a test. None of those documents silently proves the others. For warehouse, servicing advances, and repurchase risk, the most important unanswered question is the one that changes who controls cash when the ordinary route stops working.

Warehouse risk explains why securitisation begins before the notes are issued. The final investor may receive a clean pool only if the earlier funding and quality-control chain worked. Warehouse risk explains why securitisation begins before the notes are issued. The final investor may receive a clean pool only if the earlier funding and quality-control chain worked. The concept travels only when the learner can separate a transferable right from the business, asset, manager, platform, or public rule that keeps producing it.

A term security is the visible end of a pipeline whose first risks are created in the warehouse and servicing process.
